fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAREW J:

On 27 February 2026, I made a final parenting order after a contested hearing that concluded on 16 December 2025. Pursuant to that order, X (“the child”), who is nine years of age, lives with Mr Ritter (“the father”), and he has sole parental responsibility for the child. Pursuant to the final parenting order, there is a three-month moratorium preventing the mother from spending time or communicating with the child.
The father happens to live in City N in Country D (“Country D”) and when he and the child attempted to depart Australia on 4 March 2026, they were prevented from leaving Brisbane International Airport because Ms Hermann (“the mother”) had filed a fresh Initiating Application on 2 March 2026 seeking to reverse the parenting order that was made only days earlier. The filing of the mother’s application triggered the reinstatement of the Family Law Watchlist which had been removed by the order made on 27 February 2026.
On 3 March 2026, this matter was transferred to this Court from the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2).

BRIEF BACKGROUND

The mother and the father were married in 2018, separated in 2019 and divorced in 2020. They have one child together who was born on [redacted] 2016. At the time of separation, the parties were living in City N, Country D. The mother unilaterally relocated to Australia in August 2019, and a previous final parenting order was made by consent on 31 July 2020. Pursuant to that order, the child lived with the mother and was to spend time with the father. The mother repeatedly contravened the 2020 order.
The father is 46 years old. He is a British citizen and lives in City N, Country D with his wife (also a British citizen) and two children aged two and seven months respectively. The father works as a lawyer.
The mother is 44 years old. She is a dual Australian and British citizen.
The mother seeks interim and final orders in her Initiating Application filed on 2 March 2026 as follows:
Interim orders sort

The court orders that
The mother wants to restrain the removal of the child from Australia through injunction and the child remain in the mothers care - Australia whilst the father lives in [Country D].
The mother wants the fathers passport application be put on hold and a decision be made whether the child is safe to travel in the first place to [Country D] with father and its in her best interests or That the court hold the passport for [the child].
The mother unless correspondence is had in the meantime and the father adheres to the orders 27th February 2026, wants a recovery order for the child into her immediate care and airport watch re-instated for the child to not leave nor travel from Australia to [Country D] with father.

Final orders sort

Parental responsibility

TBC

1. Within 48 hours the child be delivered back into the mothers care.

2. That the child continues to live full-time with the mother.
That the mother be permitted to return the child to her primary school, up until last Friday 27th February - [J School], and the parents shall do all things and sign all documents necessary for [the child] to continue her enrolment at the school. In the event the father falls to sign. the mother has sole power to cause the continued enrolment for the child in Australia.

(As per original)
The father seeks to dismiss the mother’s application and further for an order to remove the child’s name from the Family Law Watchlist. He also seeks a harmful proceedings order prohibiting the mother from instituting any further proceedings prior to 17 March 2026 without the Court’s leave.
The mother has lodged an appeal against the 27 February 2026 order which has been listed for a preliminary hearing on 17 March 2026 to hear submissions as to why the appeal should not be summarily dismissed on the basis that the appeal grounds are incompetent.

APPLICABLE LAW

Pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), the Court is not permitted to reconsider a parenting order unless there has been a significant change in circumstances since the final parenting order and it is the child’s best interests for the parenting order to be reconsidered.
Section 65DAAA(1) of the Act provides as follows:
(1) If a final parenting order is in force in relation to a child, a court must not reconsider the final parenting order unless:
(a) the court has considered whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made; and

(b) the court is satisfied that, in all the circumstances (and taking into account whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made), it is in the best interests of the child for the final parenting order to be reconsidered.

If the Court is satisfied on a prima facie basis that there has been a significant change in circumstances, s 65DAAA(2) of the Act requires the Court to consider the factors set out in s 60CC. In addition to the matters contained in s 60CC, the Court may also take into account the further relevant considerations identified in s 65DAAA(2), which include:
(2) ...
(a) the reasons for the final parenting order and the material on which it was based;

(b) whether there is any material available that was not available to the court that made the final parenting order;

(c) the likelihood that, if the final parenting order is reconsidered, the court will make a new parenting order that affects the operation of the final parenting order in a significant way (whether by varying, discharging or suspending the final parenting order, in whole or in part, or in some other way);

(d) any potential benefit, or detriment, to the child that might result from reconsidering the final parenting order.

As the Full Court in Radecki & Radecki[1] makes clear, “[i]f there is no positive finding of changed circumstances, that is the end of the matter”.

DISCUSSION

The mother’s affidavit raises matters that were already determined at the final hearing but also makes a number of assertions upon which she relies to establish a significant change in circumstances:
(a) “There is an urgent travel ban for all Australian citizens to return to Australia in [Country D]” (emphasis omitted);

(b) “There has been an attack there”;

(c) “To the best of my knowledge, the father is yet to obtain a passport for the child ...”;

(d) “[I]f such a travel ban continues etc. a meaningful relationship mother and daughter not continue and the child suffer from no relationship mother and child now and years to come”;

(e) “The father has already breached the orders 27th February 2026” in that he has not:

(i) “allow[ed the] mother to deliver personal items to child ready for relocation ...”;

(ii) “notif[ied] the mother about fathers long term intensions [sic] for child”; and

(iii) “respond[ed] to mothers views in 48 hours”.

As part of the mother’s case she tendered, with the grateful assistance of the father’s solicitors, a bundle of printouts from various internet sites. Those 168 pages are contained in Exhibit 5.
In summary, the documents establish the following:
(a) That since the pronouncement of my parenting order on 27 February 2026 military strikes have occurred in the Middle East between the United States, Israel and Iran and that the conflict has spilled into the Middle East more generally, including [Country D];

(b) The Australian Smart Traveller website on 5 March 2026 advises persons in affected areas to avoid government buildings, military sites, energy infrastructure and US diplomatic locations and to monitor local warning systems. The advice warns that those travelling to [Country D] may not be able to leave;

(c) AA News reported on [redacted] March 2026 that “[Country D] authorities had intercepted hundreds of missiles and drones since the start of what officials described as blatant Iranian aggression. A fire near the [BB Building in City N] was contained with no injuries. Air travel continues under precautionary measures, with 48 flights per hour across [Country D] airports”;

(d) AA News reported on [redacted] March 2026 that incidents had caused [multiple] fatalities and [several] minor injuries and “[s]ome civilian facilities sustained minor to moderate material damage” and that Country D authorities “urged the public to rely on official sources and refrain from circulating rumours”;

(e) Citizens, residents and visitors in [Country D] have been advised to “[c]ontinue normal activities while exercising caution” and to “[f]ollow updates via official [Country D] government channels”;

(f) The Ministry of Education in Country D announced on [redacted] March 2026 that the spring break for schools will start early on [redacted] March 2026 with school resuming on [redacted] March 2026;

(g) The [National Government] Authority in Country D called on members of the public to adhere to official instructions; and

(h) In a report by journalist Laura Tingle, from the ABC (Australian Broadcasting Commission), she states that it is estimated that there is more than 100,000 Australian travellers and expatriates in the Gulf region, “although many are happy to stay”.

The mother’s submissions largely focussed on what she contends are the best interests of the child being returned to her rather than there being a significant change in circumstances.
Notwithstanding the mother taking the Court to the matters in Exhibit 5, the father submits that the mother’s application is not about the safety of City N because the mother is not seeking to restrain the father from leaving Australia but rather to reverse the order made only last Friday that the child live with him.
The father disputes, among other things, that there is a travel ban in place and I have no evidence before me to substantiate the mother’s assertion. Indeed, the father was to travel to City N, Country D on 4 March 2026 and was only prevented from doing so because of the mother’s application.
The father concedes that “[Country D] closed its airspace on Saturday, 28 February 2026” and as a result his booked flights with Emirates Airline for himself and the child to return to City N, Country D on 3 March 2026 were cancelled and rebooked by the airline on flights on 4 March 2026. The second flight was cancelled on the afternoon of 3 March 2026 “in circumstances where [City N] airport remained closed”. The father made alternative arrangements for the child and himself to travel to Country CC, in the Middle East, on a Singapore Airlines flight on 4 March 2026 and from there to be collected by a driver arranged by his work to drive them to City N, Country D, which the husband contends is a two-hour drive. The father and child attended Brisbane International Airport on 4 March 2026 but were prevented from leaving because of the child’s name being flagged on the Family Law Watchlist. Those circumstances do not support the mother’s assertion that there is a “travel ban”. The father has now booked further flights for himself and the child to depart Australia tonight.
The father further concedes that Iran, the United States and Israel are currently engaged in military hostilities and that on 28 February 2026 [Country D] intercepted multiple ballistic missiles and aerial threats launched toward United States military bases located in [Country D]. The father concedes that as a result of the successful interception operations, debris fell in an area of City N, Country D but not in the vicinity of his home. These matters are corroborated by Exhibit 5.
The father relies upon a public alert issued by the Minister of Interior Country D to all residents stating that the “situation is now safe in the country”. Exhibit 2 is a screen shot of the public alert. On [redacted] March 2026, the father asserts that the [National Government] Authority advised residents in Country D that they “may continue their daily activities while remaining cautious and staying informed”. Exhibit 3 is a printout of this advice.
The father deposes that his wife and two other children in City N have resumed their daily routine and activities.
The father also relies on media articles (Exhibit 4) stating that Emirates and [Airline DD] have resumed operating a “limited number of flights” and Virgin Atlantic has resumed London flights to City N and Riyadh.
The father submits that in the circumstances it is “suitably safe to return to [City N] with [the child]” and that given his evidence at trial, I should be satisfied that he would not place the child at risk.
The father deposes that as his travel plans were thwarted he and the child have had to remain in Brisbane in hotel accommodation which is obviously having a financial impact. The father contends that arrangements have been made for the child to attend a meet and greet at her new school in City N, Country D next week, that this is a time of significant change for the child and that the change in travel plans has caused confusion and disappointment for her.
The order I made on 27 February 2026, provided for the order to be explained to the child. The order obviously provides for the child to live with the father and that he be permitted to depart the Commonwealth of Australia with the child to return to his home and family in City N, Country D. That is what the child was expecting to happen prior to the interruption in her travel.

DISPOSITION

The evidence relied upon by the mother does not establish, in my view, that there has been a significant change in circumstances since the final parenting order on 27 February 2026 which would warrant a reversal in the parenting arrangements for the child nor a reconsideration of the parenting order. While there is now conflict in the Middle East which has affected Country D, advice from the local authorities in Country D have advised residents that it is safe to carry out their normal routines but has cautioned them to avoid certain areas and to heed any public alerts. The father’s home is currently in Country D and he would be there now but for the mother filing a further Initiating Application. It must be very unsettling for the child to have been told after the order was made on 27 February 2026 that she would be living with the father in City N, Country D, only to be stopped at the airport on 4 March 2026 and prevented from leaving. It is apparent from the content of the mother’s affidavit and her oral submission, that her application is really about thwarting the parenting order.
In those circumstances I am prohibited by s 65DAAA of the Act from reconsidering the parenting order.
Even if I am wrong in relation to my conclusion on the mother’s evidence, I am nevertheless not satisfied that it would be in the child’s best interests to reconsider the parenting order. The parenting order has been given effect in that the child is now living with the father. It is apparent from the mother’s material that she wishes to reagitate matters that were already determined in the recent trial.
Accordingly, her application will be dismissed.

HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS

Section 102QAC of the Act provides, as follows:
102QAC Making harmful proceedings orders

Making harmful proceedings orders
(1) A court exercising jurisdiction in proceedings under this Act may make an order (a harmful proceedings order) prohibiting a party (the first party) to the proceedings from instituting proceedings under this Act against another party to the proceedings without the leave of the court under section 102QAG, if the court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that:
(a) the other party would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party; or

(b) in the case of child-related proceedings (within the meaning of Part VII)--the child who is the subject of the proceedings would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party.

Note: Proceedings includes cross-proceedings and incidental proceedings (see subsection 4(1)).
(2) For the purposes of subsection (1), harm may include, but is not limited to, the following:
(a) psychological harm or oppression;

(b) major mental distress;

(c) a detrimental effect on the other party's capacity to care for a child;

(d) financial harm.
(3) In determining whether to make an order under subsection (1), the court may have regard to:
(a) the history of the proceedings under this Act between the first party and the other party; and

(b) whether the first party has frequently instituted or conducted proceedings against the other party in any Australian court or tribunal (including proceedings instituted (or attempted to be instituted) or conducted, and orders made, before the commencement of this section); and

(c) the cumulative effect, or any potential cumulative effect, of any harm resulting from the proceedings referred to in paragraphs (a) and (b).

(4) The court may make a harmful proceedings order on its own initiative or on application by a party to the proceedings.

(5) The court must not make a harmful proceedings order in relation to a person without hearing the person or giving the person an opportunity of being heard.

(6) An order made under subsection (1) is a final order.

Order about notifying other party in relation to application for leave etc.
(7) If the court makes an order under subsection (1), the court must also make an order as to whether the court is to notify the other party, in the event that the first party makes an application under section 102QAE for leave to institute proceedings against the other party, of either or both of the following:
(a) that the application has been made;
(b) if the application is dismissed—that the application has been dismissed.
(8) The court must have regard to the wishes of the other party in making an order under subsection (7).
(Emphasis in original)
The consequences of harmful proceedings orders are contained in s 102QAD of the Act:
102QAD Proceedings in contravention of harmful proceedings order
(1) If a person subject to a harmful proceedings order prohibiting the person from instituting proceedings under this Act in a court having jurisdiction under this Act:
(a) the person must not instigate proceedings in the court without the leave of the court under section 102QAG; and

(b) another person must not, acting in concert with the person, institute proceedings in the court without the leave of the court under section 102QAG.

(2) If the proceedings are instituted in contravention of subsection (1), the proceedings are stayed.

(3) Without limiting subsection (2), the court may make:
(a) an order declaring proceedings to which subsection (2) applied; and

(b) any other order in relation to the stayed proceedings it considers appropriate, including an order for costs.

(4) The court may make an order under section (3) on its own initiative or on the application of a person a party to the proceedings.
The father submits that the harm which the father would suffer if further proceedings are instituted would have a detrimental effect on his capacity to care for the child within the meaning of s 102QAC(2)(c) of the Act as such proceedings would trigger the child’s name again appearing on the Family Law Watchlist thus preventing his return to his home and family. Further, he submits that it would cause financial harm by having to continue to live in a hotel with the child. The father further submits that when regard is had to the history of the proceedings, the mother has already failed in similar proceedings in 2022 when she sought to relitigate the 2020 final parenting order and she has been found to repeatedly breach the previous parenting order notwithstanding being subject to a bond to comply with it.
It seems to me that a harmful proceedings order should be made prohibiting the mother from instituting further proceedings as sought by the father, i.e. prior to 17 March 2026, without the court’s prior leave.

MISCELLANEOUS

Finally, the father seeks an order that he be permitted to depart Australia notwithstanding the pending appeal. Without such an order his departure would be prevented apparently as the filing of a Notice of Appeal also triggers the child’s name appearing on the Family Law Watchlist. Such an order would therefore seem to be necessary and I will so order.
In circumstances where the mother has been wholly unsuccessful in her application, in my view this is sufficient to warrant a costs order be made against her and I will order that the mother pay the father’s costs of and incidental to her application fixed in the sum of $8,858.
